alsoKnownAs: Peacock Moss (trade overlap; some retailers treat Peacock and Spiky as the same Taxiphyllum cultivar, others as distinct)

nativeRange.habitat: Originates from Southeast Asia, where Taxiphyllum mosses grow attached to logs and rocks along the margins of ponds and streams in humid riparian zones

decorPreferences: driftwood (spiky triangular fronds showcase well on branching wood)
stainless or plastic mesh for moss walls and carpets
tied with thread/fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
gentle to moderate flow keeps debris from settling between the spiky fronds

summary: A Taxiphyllum cultivar prized for its distinctive triangle-shaped, upward-pointing spiky fronds that give the moss a denser, more architectural silhouette than Java Moss. Generally advised to attach to wood, rock, or mesh rather than bury; tolerates a wide range of light and water conditions.
careNotes: Taxiphyllum sp. 'Spiky' is one of several aquarium cultivars in the genus Taxiphyllum (alongside Java Moss T. barbieri, fw-plant-033; Flame Moss, fw-plant-132; and others). It is sold under the trade name 'Spiky Moss' and is not currently tied to a formally described species — it is treated as an undescribed/uncertain Taxiphyllum sister to T. barbieri. The trade calls it out for its distinctive triangle-shaped, upward-pointing fronds that branch into a denser, more architecturally bold mat than Java Moss's irregular bushy form. PEACOCK MOSS OVERLAP: there is real disagreement in the hobby about whether Spiky Moss and Peacock Moss are the same Taxiphyllum cultivar grown under different conditions (Fluvico explicitly states they are the same and that low light produces longer/looser growth while high light + CO2 produces denser carpeting) or two distinct cultivars (Fish Laboratory and most retailers treat them separately). This entry treats them as the same trade-name pair and documents Peacock Moss in alsoKnownAs; if a distinct Peacock Moss entry is later opened, this note should be revisited. ATTACHMENT: Spiky Moss is an epiphyte — tie or glue it to driftwood, porous rock, lava rock, or plastic/stainless mesh; do NOT bury it in substrate. Rhizoids anchor the moss to the surface over several weeks, after which thread or fishing line can be removed. LIGHTING is generally tolerant of low to medium levels (Aquarium Co-Op: 'grows well even in very low light conditions'); moderate light produces denser, more compact, and more uniformly spiked growth. CO2 is optional — the plant thrives without it, but supplementation produces denser, faster growth. GROWTH RATE is reported as slow to medium across sources, consistent with most aquarium mosses; trimming controls shape. TEMPERATURE tolerance is broad — sources cluster around 21–28 °C (Fish Laboratory: 22–28 °C / 72–82 °F; Fluvico: 22–28 °C; cold-water exposure below 15 °C should be avoided); pH 6.0–7.5 (Fish Laboratory: 6.5–7.5; Fluvico: 5.5–7.5 in the body text, 6.0–7.5 in the headline) — averaged to 6.0–7.5. Hardness gH 4–12 dGH and kH 1–8 dKH from Fish Laboratory. FERTILIZATION demand is low — the moss absorbs nutrients from the water column and does not require a nutrient-rich substrate. SHRIMP AND FRY REFUGE: like other aquarium mosses, dense Spiky Moss mats are excellent microhabitat for newly hatched fry and dwarf-shrimp larvae, which graze biofilm and microfauna from the fronds. SOURCING NOTE: Buce Plant (originally-assigned secondary) returned 404 at fetch time and was abandoned per the playbook's fail-fast rule; Fish Laboratory and Fluvico were promoted as cross-verification sources.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only in aquarium conditions: cut or pull off any healthy portion of the mat and re-attach it to a new surface — every fragment regrows. There is no need to wait for any reproductive cycle. True sexual reproduction (spore production from a sporophyte) requires emersed growth and is essentially never observed in submerged aquarium tanks.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Taxiphyllum sp. 'Spiky Moss'
primary.notes: Primary retailer product page. Used for: scientific name (Taxiphyllum sp. 'Spiky Moss'), beginner care level ('very undemanding'), low-light tolerance ('grows well even in very low light conditions'), propagation by breaking off clumps and relocating, epiphyte attachment to hardscape, distinguishing 'spiky deep green branches that extend out in multiple directions' versus Christmas Moss.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Spiky Moss Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Primary numeric-parameter source. Used for: temperature 22–28 °C (72–82 °F), pH 6.5–7.5, gH 4–12 dGH, kH 1–8 dKH, low-to-medium lighting (excess light encourages algae), CO2 optional (supplementation yields denser/faster growth), Southeast Asian origin, epiphyte attachment to hardscape, shrimp/fry refuge utility.

additional.name: Fluvico — Peacock Moss / Spiky Moss Care Guide
additional.notes: Second cross-verification source. Used for: temperature 22–28 °C, pH 5.5–7.5 (body) / 6.0–7.5 (headline), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 optional, slow growth rate, beginner difficulty, ~10 cm size, asserts that Peacock Moss and Spiky Moss are 'the exact same moss' with appearance varying by light/CO2 (basis for the alsoKnownAs entry and the careNotes 'Peacock Moss overlap' paragraph).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Taxiphyllum
additional.notes: Genus-level taxonomic authority (no species page exists for the 'Spiky' cultivar). Used for: family Hypnaceae, order Hypnales, class Bryopsida, ~44 species in genus, mat-forming creeping growth habit, broad continental distribution including Asia. Confirms that 'Spiky' is a trade cultivar not formally described as a separate Taxiphyllum species in the Wikipedia genus list.

propagation: drape over branching driftwood to showcase upward-pointing spiky fronds
